What is the Turing Test?

The Turing Test, proposed by Alan Turing in 1950, is a measure of a machine's ability to exhibit intelligent behaviour indistinguishable from that of a human.

How it works

  • A human interrogator communicates via text with both a human and a machine
  • If the interrogator cannot reliably tell which is which, the machine is said to have passed
def turing_test(interrogator, candidate):
    """Returns True if the candidate fools the interrogator."""
    responses = candidate.respond(interrogator.questions())
    return not interrogator.can_identify(responses)

Has any AI actually passed it?

In 2014, a chatbot named Eugene Goostman was claimed by some to have passed a Turing Test variant at an event organized by the University of Reading — though this claim is widely disputed by researchers who note the test conditions were lenient.

Modern LLMs like GPT-4 and Claude routinely fool humans in short exchanges, but most AI researchers consider the original Turing Test an insufficient benchmark for general intelligence.
